title: The Macro Minute: Why should investors pay more attention to the US housing market?

Happy Tuesday out there, team. 42 Macro,
your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our Macro Minute for Tuesday,
September 30th, 2025. Hope everyone's
having a great week. So, as always,
we'll start with the executive summary
from today's data morning note. If you'd
like to review the analysis supporting
these conclusions, and to see if you
need to make any changes in your
portfolio to remain on the right side of
market risk, or to get on the right side
of market risk to the extent you haven't
been following Kitcher or Dr. Mo, need
to be a client of 42 Macro.
So, today's key macro question is, why
should investors pay more attention to
the US housing market?
Our answer? The strong negative impulses
in nationwide house price inflation
featured in the latest FIFA and
Case-Shiller data support our call for
structural regime change at the Fed.
The strong negative impulse in builder
building permits, the second longest
leading indicator of the housing cycle
behind builder sentiment, also supports
our call for structural regime change at
the Fed.
Recall that the housing market is a
persistent leading indicator of the
business cycle, according to the 42
Macro business cycle model.
At best, the housing cycle is currently
mired in a moderating downturn.
Moreover, the lock-in effect continues
to restrain
the existing home sales market, which is
why new home sales are currently at an
all-time high of 17% of total.
New home supply remains cyclically and
structurally depressed, as the 3.3%
share of residential fixed investment in
GDP has only been lower in the years
immediately following the post-GFC
housing bust.
Interestingly, US households currently
expect to earn, spend, and be taxed at
above-trend rates in this income-driven
business cycle. Our credit availability
remains a noteworthy that we anticipate
will be addressed through policy
intervention in the coming quarters.
Most notably, via deregulation, lower
interest rates, and if necessary, NBS
QE.
While the lack of credit availability
has made the US more resilient to
economic volatility, it has made the
country less resilient to political
volatility.
Recall that residential mortgages
account for 2/3 of total US household
debt.
If the Fed does not become more
acquiescent to fiscal dominance, then
the housing market will not heal in
either supply or demand terms. Moreover,
with AI already eliminating jobs for
recent college graduates, more bad
monetary policy outcomes risk a lurch
towards democratic socialism on a
nationwide basis.
What is the key risk to the economy and
asset markets if the Fed does not allow
the housing market to heal because of
its arbitrary 2% inflation target? Hint,
paradigm D, which is the print phase of
the limited mix of policy responses to
the growing geopolitically driven
supply-demand imbalance in the Treasury
bond market. For example, paradigm B
Paradigm A was Biden's, you know, fiscal
largess monetary largess economy.
Paradigm B was the cut phase that was
very short-lived with the Doge
experiment.
Paradigm C is what we're currently in
now is to try to outgrow the debt
problem. And then eventually paradigm B
will be to eventually just print our way
out of the debt problem, or at least
print our way to address the debt
problem. We're probably, you know, many
years away from paradigm D, but this
paradigm C experiment could be multi-
multiple years in the making. And so, I
want investors to be aware that, you
know, having altered that view and that
theme in back in April, it's still
pretty early in the context of paradigm
C. And so, lastly, in our view, it is
highly likely the next Fed chair
understands this dynamic far better than
J. Powell. Specifically, what we mean by
the risk to the political cycle
if we do not fix this this nationwide
housing crisis or housing emergency as
as the administration is increasingly
inclined to call it. So, transition to
our 42 Macro dashboard.
As always, I'll wrap up with a question
from our community. This was not a
question, but a comment. I thought it
was important to to to highlight. Says
in title, "You've Earned My Trust."
Says, "Dear Darius, I've been following
your work for a couple of years, having
previously tracked the hedge fund you
were part of. Your intelligence and
insight have always stood out to me.
Growing up in a family of five in a
single studio apartment, I've worked
hard for every dollar I've earned, which
makes it difficult to trust others with
my money.
Over the years, I found myself aligning
with your perspectives, and though I
consider myself a thoughtful investor,
my risk-averse nature often leads me to
play it safe, sticking to cash and
hesitating to act. Your well-reasoned
approach has given me the confidence to
take calculated risks and trust your
process, even when the market
temporarily moves against me.
Thank you for your exceptional work. At
36, I see myself as a lifelong supporter
of your insights and vision. Your
mission deeply resonates with me. I hope
more people discover the value of your
products. I'll keep up the good work."
So, I just want to say super blessed by
this super very kind compliment.
You know, this is
this is exactly what we do for a living.
This is exactly what I do for a living.
This is exactly why we started this
company is to help this person, this
this this this avatar,
experience better outcomes with regards
to their investment journey, and then
ultimately, you know, their financial
freedom.
And you know, again, I spent the first
decade or 12 years of my career,
slightly over 12 years, the first 12
years of my career,
essentially using my skills, my
intellect, my thought process, my my my
my, you know, my talents, my god-given
talents to help make previously existing
rich people more rich. And that was
great. I made a ton of money doing that.
That was fantastic. But the reality is
as as I got older and older, and I
started to realize and came more and
more close to my my walk with Christ
became more closely aligned with how it
should have been, I started to realize
that I wasn't doing the right thing for
society. And so, we built this company
to essentially help address that, which
is, you know, instead of just making the
rich people richer, why don't I make
everyone richer with these skills? And
that's exactly what we've been doing
since we launched this firm, and that's
exactly what we've done been doing at an
accelerating level rate with the with
the advent of our KISS model portfolio,
which we introduced back in January of
2023. Obviously, it has demonstrably
outperformed most hedge funds since then
on the while on an absolute and
risk-adjusted basis. No no I have no
qualms about saying that. This is true.
We introduced our discussion with
Senator Novack to help our hedge fund
clients keep up with KISS back in the
fall of 2023, and they've been obviously
benefiting tremendously from the signals
that we provide there as well. So,
like I said, super proud of what we've
built here, what our team is building.
Really grateful for the opportunity to
contribute to the investment journey of
of folks like this, folks like, you
know, who grew up obviously like myself.
This person grew up in a
studio apartment with five family
members. I grew up at the best of times,
we were in the, you know, at the
absolute best of times, we were
two-bedroom housing projects with our
family of four. At the worst of times,
we were homeless, living in vans, living
in homeless shelters, living in motels.
And so, you know, I certainly have a
lot, you know, to to to to I certainly
have a lot of life experience as someone
who grew up like that.
And
naturally as a risk-averse person,
certainly as a person, but also as an
investor, you grow up and you see your
friends, you know, get murdered by gang
violence, and you grow up and you see
your family constantly getting evicted
and and and living on the streets,
you realize you develop a level of risk
aversion that is is not healthy as an
investor. And so, you know,
it's probably why I'm a risk manager for
a living, honestly. If I think about it,
you know, kind of just thinking out
loud. Like, that's probably why I'm so
focused on risk as a risk manager. But I
don't want people to be so myopically
focused on risk that they fail to
maximize upside capture in bull markets.
That to me is one of the biggest risks
to
everyday investors out there is the
failure to maximize the upside capture
in bull markets. And that failure is the
single greatest risk to your retiring on
time and comfortably. So many people get
triggered into thinking the single
greatest risk to retiring on time and
comfortably is blowing up their
portfolio in a market crash. And And to
be fair, that is true. It's a very
important risk. In my opinion, they're
equally important.
The failure to maximize upside capture
in bull markets or the failure to
adequately manage risk heading into a
bear market. Those in my opinion are
identical.
Risk works in both directions, and it's
always a function of your legacy
position. And so, this is why I preach
every single day here in these Macro
Minutes, and every single thing that we
do here 42 Macro, that our number one
job as investors is to stay on the right
side of market risk.
It's not to be, you know, 100% bullish
or 100% bearish, or to to overtrade our
accounts, or do this, or do that. It's
just to to
participate in what the market is trying
to price in, and/or get to the safety of
cash and on the sidelines when the
market is trying to price in something
hazardous to your financial health. So,
I think we do a great job for, you know,
customers like this, and obviously doing
a great job for myself. I use KISS to
manage my entire liquid net worth as
someone who's highly risk-averse and is
highly convinced and highly certain that
I am not going back to the poorhouse,
the poorhouse where I where I I came
from.
You know, you can trust that someone who
eats what they cook to such a high
degree that this stuff is is is highly
highly effective at managing risk
for people folks like myself and folks
like this customer. So, I'll stop
rambling and just say thank you to this
person. Thank you to all of our
many thousands of members around the
world on team 42 who uh
showered me with this kind of wonderful
you know, feedback that gives me the the
power and and and the energy to to to to
to to to to to do this grind on a daily
basis. And I tell you what, it is a
grind. So,
I appreciate y'all. Just say thank you.
I'm wrapping up there. Catch you back
here tomorrow. Cheers.
